title: Intelligence Briefing Dashboard
subtitle: Weekly Conflict Monitor · All Regions
sources: Reuters · AP News · Al Jazeera · ACLED · US Embassy Alerts · LiveUAMap · BNO News · ISW · British Defence Intelligence · Kyiv Post · Sudan Tribune · Irrawaddy · CNN · NBC · CBS · Fox News · HRW · UN News · CFR · Times of Israel · NPR · CNBC · Atlantic Council · Sudan Tribune · US State Dept · OHCHR · Wikipedia · Washington Post

regions.name: Middle East
overview: The 60-day US-Iran ceasefire collapsed — the US conducted multiple strike waves on Iranian territory (Bushehr, Bandar Abbas, Chabahar, Jask, Konarak, Kish, Qeshm, Abu Musa) and reinstated a naval blockade of Iranian ports effective 14 July, while Iran retaliated with missiles/drones against Jordan, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar and disabled two UAE-flagged tankers off Oman.

A new front opened when Yemen's Houthis broke their four-year truce with Saudi Arabia, striking Abha International Airport (13-14 Jul) after accusing Riyadh of blocking a Houthi delegation's flight from Tehran; Houthi officials have since threatened to 'besiege' Saudi Arabia.

Gaza's ceasefire remains nominal — cumulative deaths since the truce began have surpassed 1,100 with 3,500+ wounded, and Israel is expanding its 'Yellow Zone' buffer; Lebanon's ceasefire is similarly undermined by near-daily Israeli strikes in the south.

Syria's investigation into the two Damascus bombings produced arrests of an ISIL-affiliated cell (9 Jul), though full details remain pending, while Israeli settler encroachment into Syrian and West Bank territory continues alongside a reported 560% rise in nationalist-crime probes.

countries.name: Gaza
incidents: 1 Jul — Al-Hilu station, Gaza City: Israeli drone strike killed at least 3 Palestinians despite the ceasefire.
9 Jul — Nuseirat/Gaza-wide: Israeli strikes killed 12 and wounded 20 over a single 24-hour period.
13 Jul — Gaza-wide: Israeli forces killed 3 and wounded 15 as they expanded the 'Yellow Zone' buffer area.
countries.coa: The October 2025 ceasefire remains nominally in force but is hollow — cumulative deaths since the truce began have surpassed 1,100 with 3,500+ wounded. Israel's expansion of the 'Yellow Zone' buffer adds fresh territorial friction. CRITICAL maintained; watch whether third-party monitoring gains traction and whether the daily strike tempo falls.

countries.name: Lebanon
incidents: 6 Jul — Nabatieh al-Fawqa: Israeli drone strike on a vehicle killed at least 4, including a school principal, her mother, a domestic worker and a Syrian national.

7-14 Jul — Southern Lebanon: Intermittent Israeli strikes continued despite the 21 June ceasefire; over 4,000 have been killed by Israeli strikes in Lebanon since March 2026 and 1 million+ remain displaced.

9-14 Jul — regional spillover risk: The collapse of the US-Iran ceasefire and reopened Houthi-Saudi front raised renewed risk Hezbollah could be drawn into a wider Iran-aligned exchange, though no major Hezbollah-initiated attack was confirmed this week.
countries.coa: Unchanged — continued lethal Israeli strikes despite the formal truce, and the collapse of the wider US-Iran ceasefire, keep alive the risk Hezbollah is pulled off the sidelines. CRITICAL maintained.

countries.name: Iran
incidents: 9 Jul — Bushehr/Bandar Abbas/Chabahar/Jask/Konarak/Abu Musa: The US struck military and nuclear-adjacent infrastructure over three consecutive nights as the 60-day ceasefire collapsed; multiple Iranian servicemen and civilians were killed.
13 Jul — Bandar Abbas/Kish/Qeshm/Abu Musa: Third consecutive night of US strikes; Iran said it struck and disabled two UAE-flagged tankers in the Strait of Hormuz, killing 1 and wounding 8.
14 Jul — Gulf-wide: The US confirmed resumption of a naval blockade of Iranian ports effective 1600 ET; Iran vowed to assert control over the Strait of Hormuz.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, trending worse — the 60-day ceasefire has broken down and a multi-night US strike campaign plus a renewed naval blockade signal a return toward all-out war. Watch Omani/Qatari/Pakistani mediation efforts and post-Khamenei succession dynamics as the key variables.

countries.name: Yemen
incidents: 13 Jul — Sanaa International Airport: Saudi airstrikes reportedly blocked a Houthi delegation plane returning from Khamenei's funeral in Tehran.
13-14 Jul — Abha International Airport, Saudi Arabia: Houthis retaliated with ballistic missiles and drones, ending the four-year Saudi-Houthi truce.
14 Jul — regional: Houthi political bureau member Mohammed al-Bukhaiti threatened to impose a 'siege' on Saudi Arabia.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The collapse of the four-year Saudi-Houthi truce is a major new escalation layered onto the wider Iran war — watch for a Saudi ground/air response and renewed Houthi threats to Red Sea shipping in solidarity with Tehran.

countries.name: Israel
incidents: 7-13 Jul — nationwide: Israel remained a principal target in the widening US-Iran war (missile/drone exposure from Iran, Houthis and Lebanon) as the ceasefire collapsed.
8 Jul — West Bank-wide: Haaretz reported 'Jewish nationalist crime' probes up 560% since 2019, with very few indictments, amid continued settler violence.
11 Jul — Sinjil, West Bank: Israeli settlers attacked CNN and other foreign journalists covering an anniversary; 4 arrested amid at least 20 settler-violence incidents that weekend.
1-10 Jul — West Bank: Over 3,200 Palestinians have been displaced by settler attacks/demolitions since January; 67 displaced and 24 structures razed in the first 10 days of July alone.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Israel remains directly exposed as a primary combatant in the widening Iran war (Iran, Houthi and Lebanese threat vectors) while West Bank settler violence continues to climb, per Haaretz's reported 560% rise in nationalist-crime probes.

countries.name: Iraq
incidents: 9 Jul — near Erbil: A drone attack hit a camp of the Iranian-Kurdish opposition group PJAK; no fatalities reported, responsible party unconfirmed.

9-13 Jul — Iraq-wide/Kurdistan Region: Iran-aligned militias (Kataib Hezbollah, Kataib Sayyid al-Shuhada, Harakat Hezbollah al-Nujaba) formally joined the wider war, firing 'hundreds' of missiles/drones at US-linked facilities since late February, increasingly hitting civilian infrastructure.
14 Jul — Washington: PM Ali al-Zaidi met President Trump at the White House; Trump called Iran a 'big burden' on Iraq and touted new US oil investment.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Iraq remains a secondary but active battleground for Iran-aligned militia attacks on US-linked sites without direct state-on-state conflict; the al-Zaidi-Trump channel offers a possible avenue to limit strikes on Iraqi soil.

countries.name: Saudi Arabia
incidents: 13 Jul — Sanaa International Airport, Yemen: Saudi airstrikes reportedly blocked a Houthi delegation flight returning from Tehran.
13-14 Jul — Abha International Airport: Houthis struck with ballistic missiles and drones in direct retaliation, ending a four-year truce; no casualties reported.
14 Jul — regional: A senior Houthi official threatened to impose a 'siege' on Saudi Arabia.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, trending toward escalation. The collapse of the Houthi truce is a significant new threat vector layered onto existing exposure from the wider Iran war; watch southern airports (Abha, Najran, Jizan) for further strikes.

countries.name: Syria
incidents: 2 Jul — Midan, Damascus: A bombing near the main judicial complex killed 10 and wounded 21.
7 Jul — Four Seasons Hotel area, Damascus: Twin IEDs detonated during French President Macron's state visit, killing 1 and wounding 36; Macron was unharmed.
9 Jul — Damascus and countryside: Syria's Interior Ministry announced the arrest of the entire ISIL-affiliated cell behind both bombings following coordinated raids.
6 Jul — Daraa/Mount Hermon: Reports continued of a new Israeli settlement established inside Syrian territory, with the IDF detaining Israeli civilians attempting further crossings.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Two mass-casualty Damascus bombings within a week, even with arrests announced, indicate a still-active ISIL-linked network; continued unauthorized Israeli settlement expansion into Syrian territory adds a second axis of instability. Watch whether the investigation's findings hold up and whether further cells are uncovered.

countries.name: Egypt
incidents: 3 Jul — nationwide: Egypt announced a shift to domestically developed encryption for Air Defense Forces communications, citing rising cyber/drone/electronic-warfare threats in the regional environment.
Ongoing — Sinai: No major kinetic terrorism incidents were reported this week; the peninsula has remained free of major attacks for three years per travel-advisory sources.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No dated kinetic incident on Egyptian soil this week; the air-defense cyber-hardening move reads as preparatory posturing for potential regional spillover from the widening Iran and Yemen fronts rather than a response to a direct incident.

countries.name: Jordan
incidents: 9 Jul — Azraq military base/Prince Hassan Air Base: Iran fired 10 ballistic missiles; Jordan intercepted 8, with Iran claiming damage to a command center and drone hangars; no casualties or major damage confirmed by Jordanian authorities.
9 Jul — Jordanian airspace: A separate report noted Jordan intercepted 4 additional missiles transiting Jordanian territory.
Ongoing — Jordan has intercepted 261 of 281 Iranian projectiles aimed at the country since late February 2026, underscoring its role as a recurring target.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. The high interception rate with zero casualties on 9 Jul is a positive data point, but Jordan's role as a recurring Iranian target and continued US basing at Azraq keep it at elevated risk; any future unintercepted strike would justify an upgrade to CRITICAL.

countries.name: Libya
incidents: 13 Jul — Al-Zawiya: Armed clashes erupted between rival armed groups in several parts of the city, forcing closure of the coastal 'Refinery Road.'
13 Jul — Sirte: Rival Libyan military chiefs from east and west held landmark face-to-face unification talks.
Ongoing — Misrata-based armed groups rejected the US-backed 'Boulos initiative' (oil investment for unity), while deadly militia clashes continued flaring in Tripoli.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Localized militia clashes in Zawiya and Tripoli persist alongside a genuine, high-level political unification track emerging from the Sirte talks — a mixed but not sharply worsening picture. Watch for spoiler violence timed to disrupt the unification process.

countries.name: Tunisia
incidents: 8 Jul — Tunis: A court sentenced 21 people, including Ennahda leaders and former officials, to 12-35 years on terrorism/state-security charges; exiled former Ennahda president Rached Ghannouchi received 14 years in absentia.

8 Jul — Geneva: UN experts and civil society raised alarm at the UN Human Rights Council over Tunisia's 'intensifying human rights crisis' and repression of journalists, opposition figures and lawyers.
Early-mid Jul — Djerba/Tunis: At least 14 people were arrested and at least 6 sentenced under Article 230 of the penal code.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No terrorism/kinetic incident this week, but the intensifying political crackdown — mass sentencing, activist arrests, UN alarm — is a notable political-stability risk factor worth close monitoring in the coming weeks.

regions.name: Ukraine/Russia
overview: Ukraine's deep-strike refinery campaign became strategically decisive: Omsk (7 Jul), Saratov (8 Jul) and Syzran (12 Jul) were all hit this week, striking the last of Russia's top-11 gasoline refineries and pushing an estimated 42.74% of national refining capacity offline; the fuel crisis now affects 78 of 83 Russian regions, several under formal states of emergency.

Russia's strike tempo against Kyiv and Ukrainian port infrastructure stayed at the war's highest intensity — the 6 Jul Kyiv attack killed at least 26, and a sustained two-night bombardment of Chornomorsk (11-12 Jul) knocked out major grain-export capacity, signaling a shift toward economic/logistics targets as Moscow's own energy sector degrades.

The Kostiantynivka front remains contested despite Russian claims of capture, illustrating the war's grinding character even as both sides escalate strategic strikes elsewhere; Ukraine's Rada extended martial law and mobilization to 31 October.

Belarus quietly complied with Kyiv's ultimatum to shut down Russian drone-relay stations by 2 July — a rare de-escalatory data point — while Moldova's new PM nominee Vasile Tofan faces a 15-day confirmation clock and Georgia's protest movement passed 590+ consecutive days under a still-tightening crackdown.

countries.name: Ukraine
incidents: 6 Jul — Kyiv: Russia fired 351 drones and 68 missiles overnight, killing at least 26 in the second mass missile attack on the capital in four days.

11-12 Jul — Chornomorsk: Russian cruise missiles struck the port over two consecutive nights, destroying grain and oil terminals (~45,000 tonnes wheat, 9,000 tonnes sunflower oil lost); port operations were suspended.
12 Jul — nationwide: Russia struck with 13 missiles and 115 drones overnight, killing 3 in Kryvyi Rih; Ukrainian forces struck 21 Russian logistics vessels in the Sea of Azov.

14 Jul — Kyiv/Kherson: Russia launched 135 drones and 10 missiles overnight, damaging 16 facilities in Kyiv and injuring 4 civilians in Kherson; Ukraine struck 11 more Azov vessels and hit the Gazprom Neftekhim Salavat petrochemical complex in Bashkortostan.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, deepening. Kyiv and Ukrainian port infrastructure remain under the war's highest-intensity bombardment while Ukraine's refinery campaign has evolved into a systematic, near-daily operation that struck the last of Russia's top-11 refineries this week (Omsk, Saratov, Syzran). Kostiantynivka remains genuinely contested despite Russian capture claims.

countries.name: Russia
incidents: 7 Jul — Omsk: Ukrainian drones struck Russia's largest refinery (~10% of national refining capacity) 2,500km from Ukraine, halting operations — the last of Russia's top-11 gasoline refineries to be hit.
8 Jul — Saratov: A refinery was hit and set ablaze, with its only crude distillation unit reportedly damaged; two tankers were also struck.
12 Jul — Syzran, Samara Oblast: Ukraine's General Staff confirmed a drone strike on the refinery, with explosions and fire reported.

Ongoing — nationwide: Ukraine's General Staff estimated 42.74% of Russian refining capacity disabled; the fuel crisis now affects 78 of 83 regions, with six regions reporting total gas-station shortages and Oryol introducing odd/even license-plate rationing (10 Jul).
countries.coa: HIGH maintained; the fuel crisis has become genuinely nationwide with real civil-unrest potential given rationing and price shocks. Watch for intensified Kremlin measures to manage domestic strain (rationing expansion, export bans, subsidies) and for retaliatory escalation against Ukrainian energy/port infrastructure.

countries.name: Belarus
incidents: 6 Jul — Minsk: Lukashenko told military academy graduates Belarus faces a 'hybrid war' and reaffirmed troops will not be sent to fight in Ukraine, while stressing continued military buildup.

Late Jun-2 Jul — nationwide: Following a Ukrainian ultimatum, Lukashenko shut down Russian drone-relay stations used to guide Shahed/Geran attacks into western Ukraine; no further Russian drone transits via Belarus were reported afterward.

Ongoing — Suwałki Gap/border: NATO's 'Gallant Boar 2026' exercises continued near the Gap; Belarus reportedly drafted a 2026-2039 state armaments program signaling long-term military expansion despite the relay de-escalation.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Lukashenko's compliance with the drone-relay ultimatum is a rare de-escalatory data point, but continued military buildup plans and NATO eastern-flank hardening reflect a fragile equilibrium rather than genuine disengagement from the conflict.

countries.name: Moldova
incidents: 11 Jul — Chișinău: President Sandu nominated businessman Vasile Tofan as PM; a 15-day confirmation clock is now running, with the pro-Russian Party of Socialists declaring it will not legitimize the process.

12-13 Jul — Copanca, Căușeni district: A Russian Shahed/Geran-type drone crossed into Moldovan airspace and crashed amid Russian strikes on Ukraine's Odesa region; no casualties, warhead reportedly did not detonate.
13 Jul — Chișinău: Moldova's Foreign Ministry summoned Russia's ambassador and issued a formal protest note over the sovereignty violation.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. A second PM nominee (Tofan) now faces a 15-day confirmation clock and an outright opposition boycott, while a Russian drone incursion this week — the latest in a pattern tied to strikes on nearby Odesa — reinforces the direct spillover risk. Watch whether Tofan secures confirmation by roughly 26 July.

countries.name: Georgia
incidents: 11 Jul — Tbilisi: Pro-EU protests outside parliament passed their 591st consecutive day, with a march dedicated to Georgian fighters killed in Ukraine.

Early-mid Jul — Tbilisi: Opposition figure Giorgi Targamadze became the 8th figure arrested in proceedings tied to boycotting the ruling Georgian Dream party's parliamentary commission, part of a pattern Amnesty International calls a 'coordinated system to crush dissent.'
Ongoing — Tbilisi/Brussels: EU sanctions debate over Georgia remains stalled due to Hungarian and Slovak vetoes, while courts continue sentencing protesters under the automatic-detention law.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. The risk remains chronic and structural — a sustained, institutionalized crackdown apparatus continues alongside a protest movement approaching its 600th day, while EU enforcement remains gridlocked.

countries.name: Armenia
incidents: Early Jul — Armenia-Azerbaijan border: Roughly 12km of the border is now delimited and demarcated, the only stretch where both sides' forces serve without helmets/body armor; PM Pashinyan called the development 'historic.'
1-2 Jul — Brussels/Yerevan: European Commission President von der Leyen reaffirmed EU support for the Armenia-Azerbaijan peace framework.

Ongoing — Yerevan: A draft constitution removing territorial-claim language Baku demands be excised is to be publicized by year-end, with a referendum still slated for 2027, delaying formal signature of the peace agreement.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. No kinetic incidents this window; the peace process continues advancing incrementally via border delimitation and diplomatic engagement, though formal signature remains tied to Armenia's 2027 constitutional referendum timeline.

countries.name: Azerbaijan
incidents: 13 Jul — Shusha: President Aliyev said Azerbaijan is considering full withdrawal from the Council of Europe over the 2024 revocation of Baku's PACE voting rights.
1 Jul — Shusha: Aliyev held an expanded meeting with European Commission President von der Leyen, describing recent Baku-Brussels engagement as significant.
Ongoing — energy: Azerbaijan continued expanding gas exports, now supplying 16 countries including recently-started deliveries to Germany and Austria.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. No conflict-related incidents this week; the Council of Europe withdrawal threat is a diplomatic, not security, development worth tracking as a bellwether for Azerbaijan's alignment with European institutions.

regions.name: Sahel
overview: The JNIM-Azawad Liberation Front coalition in Mali demonstrated it can strike Gao, Mopti, Ségou and Timbuktu regions simultaneously while besieging a major garrison — Anéfis was recaptured by FAMa/Africa Corps on 9-10 July after a costly relief operation, but Kidal remains lost and the offensive is assessed as the most severe phase of the conflict since 2012.

Niger's crisis widened from insurgent pressure into an unresolved, unconfirmed internal loyalty crisis — Presidential Guard forces reinforced the State House in Niamey (3-4 Jul) amid reports of an armored unit refusing frontline deployment, compounding the sustained JNIM/ISSP threat across Tillabéri.

Sudan's El Obeid is now explicitly flagged by UN bodies as at imminent risk of replicating the El Fasher atrocities — the UN Human Rights Council mandated a Fact-Finding Mission (6-8 Jul) after documenting roughly ten days of drone strikes killing at least 50 civilians and RSF control of nearly all routes into the city.

Burkina Faso formally ruptured diplomatic relations with France (12-13 Jul), removing a key intelligence-sharing channel just as reporting confirmed the army is preventing civilians from leaving jihadist-besieged towns — part of a broader AES-bloc pivot away from Western partners even as threat complexity rises across the region.

countries.name: Mali
incidents: 4 Jul — Gao, Aguelhok, Anéfis: JNIM and the Azawad Liberation Front launched a coordinated multi-front assault, encircling and cutting off the Anéfis garrison.

9-10 Jul — Anéfis, Kidal Region: A FAMa/Africa Corps relief column fought its way roughly 300km from Gao and recaptured the town after heavy losses on both sides; Kidal remains under JNIM-FLA control.

5-9 Jul — Kouakourou, Sofara, Léré: JNIM struck three additional army positions and targeted pro-government Dozo militia across Mopti, Ségou and Timbuktu regions as part of the same nationwide offensive.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Anéfis was recaptured, but analysts assess the 4 July offensive as the largest since the 2012 rebellion — JNIM-FLA demonstrated renewed capacity for simultaneous multi-region operations just three months after the April wave. Kidal remains lost and the Gao-Kidal corridor stays highly contested.

countries.name: Niger
incidents: 3-4 Jul — Niamey: The Presidential Guard, backed by armored vehicles, sealed off and reinforced the State House overnight following unconfirmed reports of an armored FAN unit refusing deployment to frontline Tillabéri positions (Inatès, Chinagodrar); the government has issued no official confirmation.
Ongoing — Tillabéri: The near-monthly pattern of mass-casualty garrison attacks (Diaguru, 21 soldiers killed, 2 Jul) continues to strain army capacity across the region.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Even absent a fresh confirmed attack this week, the unresolved and unconfirmed loyalty crisis inside the security apparatus itself, layered onto sustained JNIM/ISSP pressure across Tillabéri, Tahoua and Diffa, represents a compounding risk to regime stability beyond territorial control. Treat continued government silence as a negative indicator.

countries.name: Burkina Faso
incidents: 12-13 Jul — Ouagadougou/Paris: France completed withdrawal of its remaining diplomatic staff after Burkina Faso severed diplomatic relations; Burkinabè diplomats in France received expulsion notices with a seven-day deadline.

30 Jun-3 Jul — Gayéri, Solhan, Sebba: Coordinated insurgent attacks were met with a large counter-insurgency response; Burkinabè forces claimed 400+ militants killed against 3 confirmed soldier deaths.
6 Jul — nationwide: A New Humanitarian investigation found the army is preventing civilians from leaving jihadist-besieged towns, with an estimated 800,000 people isolated nationally.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The formal rupture with France removes a key intelligence-sharing channel just as the UN Human Rights Office has been forced to close its Burkina Faso operations, and credible reporting of the army trapping civilians in besieged towns points to worsening humanitarian conditions and eroding state legitimacy.

countries.name: Sudan
incidents: 6 Jul — El Obeid: RSF now controls all routes into the city except the eastern approach; roughly 500,000 residents, including 105,000 IDPs, remain trapped.

6-8 Jul — Geneva/El Obeid: The UN Human Rights Council mandated its Fact-Finding Mission to investigate violations in El Obeid, warning the city 'must not become the next crime scene' as RSF replicates El Fasher siege tactics.

Early-mid Jul — El Obeid/North Kordofan: The UN documented roughly ten consecutive days of drone strikes killing at least 50 civilians, including 13 killed in a single strike on a vehicle en route to a wedding; over 300 people were killed along the Bara corridor in the first half of July.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. El Obeid has not fallen — SAF still holds the city — but UN bodies are now explicitly pre-empting an El Fasher-style atrocity scenario given near-total encirclement, relentless drone strikes on civilians and a documented fatality surge. Treat humanitarian access as actively compromised.

countries.name: Chad
incidents: 9 Jul — assessment: The International Crisis Group flagged Chad as at risk of both intensified RSF cross-border incursions/drone strikes in the east and an overstretched military response to Boko Haram in the Lake province state-of-emergency zone.

Ongoing — eastern Chad/Adré corridor: The $21 million IOM response plan for Sudanese refugees is only 19% funded, with warnings assistance could collapse after October absent new funding, against a backdrop of 935,870+ Sudanese refugees now in Chad.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. No new confirmed Lake province incident this week, but structural risk factors — Sudan spillover, an underfunded refugee response, and continued Boko Haram pressure — remain fully active. A Kordofan collapse would likely drive fresh refugee flows and possible spillover into Chadian territory via the Adré corridor.

countries.name: Mauritania
incidents: Ongoing — nationwide: The underlying repression pattern from June's custodial-death protests (Oumar Diop) — internet shutdowns, mass arrests — is corroborated as continuing and unresolved, per IFEX and Amnesty reporting.
Ongoing — nationwide: Continued limitations on freedom of assembly, including banned opposition rallies and dispersed commemorations.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. No fresh dated incident this week, but the underlying repression pattern is assessed as continuing rather than resolved — reduced headline coverage likely reflects the chilling effect of prior internet shutdowns rather than genuine calm. Recommend downgrading only after independent judicial review of custodial deaths and no further protest-related fatalities over 30 days.

countries.name: Nigeria
incidents: 5-13 Jul — Talakawa, Chibok LGA and environs: Rescue operations freed additional Lassa school captives in stages (6 more on 5 Jul, then 4 adults and 2 infants), but roughly 36 students/staff remain unaccounted for three weeks after the 30 June abduction.

11 Jul — Cross Kauwa, Kukawa LGA: The Nigerian Army repelled a large-scale ISWAP night assault, recovering a camcorder revealing foreign ISWAP commanders (a Palestinian trainer, a Moroccan doctor) embedded with the group; 2 soldiers wounded.

7-9 Jul — Ruwan Bore District/Talata Mafara, Zamfara State: Armed bandits stormed villages and rustled cattle; joint police/military operations rescued a kidnapped INEC staff member and recovered livestock.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The Lassa abduction crisis remains open with roughly 36 still missing, while ISWAP sustained a multi-axis offensive tempo around Maiduguri with foreign-linked command elements now confirmed. A parallel banditry/kidnapping crisis in Zamfara adds a second high-intensity front in the Northwest.

regions.name: Asia-Pacific
overview: China's multi-domain grey-zone campaign entered a new phase — its first-ever open-ocean submarine-launched ballistic missile test (6 Jul) coincided with Joint Sea-2026 drills with Russia at Qingdao, a repeat Senkaku coast guard standoff with Japan, and continued Scarborough Shoal pressure on the Philippines — signaling deliberate, calibrated escalation across at least three fronts simultaneously.

Pakistan is now fighting a three-front internal crisis: a Balochistan insurgency surge (42 killed, Operation Shaban launched 5 Jul, 125 militants claimed killed by 14 Jul), an active cross-border drone war with Afghanistan, and a deepening uprising in Pakistan-administered Kashmir (600+ activists detained, a 15 Jul march planned) — the most acute multi-front instability in the region this week.

Myanmar's junta continues losing ground on all three fronts — Rakhine near-total loss to the Arakan Army (only Kyaukphyu, Manaung and Sittwe townships remain under junta control), Sagaing under sustained aerial bombardment of civilians, and a major Ayeyarwady offensive to reopen the Pathein-Monywa highway — while the AA increasingly behaves as a de facto sovereign government.

Indonesia's Papua conflict escalated with the killing of an American aid pilot by TPNPB fighters (2 Jul), the first foreign national killed by separatists this year, even as claimant states around the South China Sea deepen multilateral cooperation to counterbalance Beijing.

countries.name: North Korea
incidents: 6-9 Jul — Pyongyang: Kim Jong Un chaired a Central Military Commission meeting on expanding nuclear forces 'quantitatively and qualitatively' and modernizing naval/shipyard capacity.
9 Jul — Pyongyang: KCNA condemned expanding South Korea-Japan military logistics cooperation as a 'foolish act courting self-destruction.'

Ongoing — DMZ: Fortification continued faster than South Korean planners expected, with fencing now within roughly 80m of the Military Demarcation Line and troop mobilization roughly 5x higher than the same period last year; Seoul's Defense Ministry formally called this an armistice violation.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Sustained nuclear-force expansion rhetoric, accelerated DMZ militarization years ahead of prior schedule, and hostile posture toward Seoul-Tokyo cooperation show continued military build-up with no de-escalation signal.

countries.name: Myanmar
incidents: 4 Jul — Yekyi Township, Ayeyarwady: The Arakan Army captured two junta outposts as part of the AA-led coalition's push over the Pathein-Monywa highway.
6 Jul — Phaungpyin Township, Sagaing: Junta fighter jets bombed civilian/gold-mining villages, killing one woman and injuring roughly 10.
6-8 Jul — Chaung-U/Homalin Townships, Sagaing: Junta forces shelled villages with heavy weapons and advanced columns toward Shwe Pyi Aye town.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The junta continues fighting simultaneously in Rakhine (near-total loss), Sagaing (aerial bombardment of civilians) and Ayeyarwady (highway offensive), stretching its capacity; only Kyaukphyu, Manaung and Sittwe townships remain under junta control in Rakhine.

countries.name: Pakistan
incidents: 4-8 Jul — Balochistan (Ziarat, Lasbela): Coordinated militant assaults killed 42, including 18 abducted police executed near the Mangi Dam project; Pakistan launched 'Operation Shaban' (5 Jul), claiming 125 militants killed by 14 Jul.

1 Jul — Saranan, Balochistan/Khyber Pakhtunkhwa: Afghan Taliban forces launched drone strikes into Pakistani territory targeting an alleged ISIS site; Pakistan said it shot down four drones, with two civilians injured near a school.

5-13 Jul — Pakistan-administered Kashmir (Muzaffarabad/Rawalakot): A mass crackdown followed a JAAC leader's arrest and 600+ activist detentions; JAAC threatened a march on Muzaffarabad for 15 July after unrest that has left 20+ dead since early June.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, intensifying. Simultaneous large-scale Balochistan insurgency, an active cross-border war with Afghanistan, and a deepening political crisis in Pakistan-administered Kashmir represent a three-front internal security emergency; watch the 15 Jul Muzaffarabad march and 27 Jul Azad Kashmir election.

countries.name: India
incidents: 6 Jul — Ukhrul district, Manipur: A sophisticated two-hour ambush on an Assam Rifles convoy killed two personnel, assessed as fallout from a June split within a Myanmar-based Naga insurgent faction.
12-13 Jul — Kanto Sabal village, Imphal West, Manipur: Arson attacks destroyed abandoned Meitei-community houses; two arrests made amid fears of renewed ethnic unrest.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained, with rising risk flagged. The Manipur ambush shows increased insurgent tactical sophistication tied to a Naga faction split, while fresh arson in Imphal West raises fears of renewed Meitei-Kuki communal tension; the LAC with China remains comparatively calm.

countries.name: China
incidents: 6 Jul — South China Sea to Pacific: China conducted its first-ever open-ocean submarine-launched ballistic missile test, overflying northern Luzon before splashdown between Nauru and Tonga.
6-13 Jul — Qingdao, Yellow Sea: 'Joint Sea-2026' exercises with Russia included a Russian task group conducting anti-submarine, air-defense and artillery drills.
7 Jul — Senkaku Islands: A Japan-China coast guard standoff occurred over a Japanese fishing vessel, the second such confrontation since 10 June.
4-6 Jul — Taiwan Strait: MND recorded elevated PLA activity, including 30 sorties on 5 Jul with 26 crossing the median line.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, trending toward the top of the band. The combination of a first-ever Pacific SLBM test, simultaneous joint drills with Russia, a repeat Senkaku confrontation and continued Taiwan Strait pressure represents a broadening, multi-theater coercion campaign.

countries.name: Taiwan
incidents: 4-6 Jul — Taiwan Strait/ADIZ: Elevated PLA sorties, including 30 on 5 Jul with 26 crossing the median line, coincided with China's SLBM test and Joint Sea-2026 launch.
13 Jul — nationwide: Taiwan's military ran five-day pre-Han Kuang joint exercises focused on decentralized operations ahead of the annual Han Kuang exercise.

Ongoing — Taiwan's National Security Council publicly warned China is pursuing incremental 'new normal' expansion below the threshold of war, urging coordinated response with Japan and the Philippines.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Continued PLA pressure and preparation for the Han Kuang exercise reflect sustained but still sub-crisis tension; monitor ADIZ incursion cyclicality against political triggers.

countries.name: Philippines
incidents: Early-mid Jul — Scarborough Shoal: A Philippine Navy vessel encountered four PLA warships in a radio-challenge standoff, coinciding with the conclusion of the 'Salaknib 2026' joint exercise.

~10 Jul — Scarborough Shoal: Filipino fishermen reported being driven from the shoal as Chinese coast guard pressure continued; China submitted new baseline 'territorial waters' charts to the UN, which Manila called 'baseless.'
12 Jul — multilateral: The Philippines, Japan, the US and 11 other countries issued a joint statement marking the 10th anniversary of the 2016 South China Sea arbitral ruling.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. Persistent Scarborough Shoal pressure and fisherman displacement continue, offset partly by strengthened multilateral diplomatic backing against China's new baseline claims.

countries.name: South Korea
incidents: 9 Jul — Seoul: South Korea responded diplomatically to North Korea's condemnation of Seoul-Tokyo defense cooperation.
13 Jul — nationwide: South Korea's military disclosed a record number of cyberattack attempts against its networks.
14 Jul — DMZ: Seoul publicly voiced concern over accelerating North Korean border fortification activity.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, driven primarily by North Korea's fortification activity, record cyberattack volume and rhetoric against Seoul-Tokyo cooperation.

countries.name: Vietnam
incidents: Late Jun-ongoing — South China Sea: Chinese vessel transits within 10nm of Vietnamese-held features rose to 89 over the past 12 months, versus 50 three years prior.
Late Jun — Manila: President To Lam visited the Philippines, elevating the Vietnam-Philippines relationship to an 'enhanced' strategic partnership focused on South China Sea cooperation.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Rising Chinese vessel activity near Vietnamese features indicates a slow-building maritime friction trend without escalation to confrontation this week; watch the vessel-transit trend line for further acceleration.

countries.name: Japan
incidents: 7 Jul — Senkaku Islands: A coast guard standoff with China occurred, the second such incident since 10 June.
7-8 Jul — Ankara: Defense Minister Koizumi held a run of bilateral defense ministerials on NATO summit sidelines.
Ongoing — Tokyo: A senior ruling-party lawmaker publicly proposed Japan acquire at least 8 nuclear-powered submarines, reflecting growing unease over US security-commitment reliability.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained, watch for upgrade. Senkaku confrontations are becoming a near-monthly pattern, and Japan's own posture is shifting toward more assertive self-reliant defense planning.

countries.name: Indonesia
incidents: 2 Jul — Yahukimo, Highland Papua: TPNPB fighters shot and killed American pilot Nikolas Gosselin and burned his aircraft, calling the killing a 'message' over pilots ferrying Indonesian troops; seven passengers survived.
10 Jul — Jakarta: Police raids linked to corruption/money-laundering investigations exposed a public rift between police and military/prosecutorial agencies.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained, validated by this week's events. The killing of a foreign pilot marks a deadlier escalation of the Papua conflict and a more internationally sensitive TPNPB tactic; maintain no-travel guidance for Highland/Central Papua.

countries.name: Thailand
incidents: Early Jul — Chong Krang area, Surin province: Cambodia alleged Thai soldiers crossed the border and attacked a Cambodian position; Thai military disputed this, attributing injuries to an accidental Cambodian landmine detonation.
Ongoing — Chanthaburi province: Thailand's National Security Council is reviewing whether to revoke 2000/2001 border-demarcation MOUs with Cambodia.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. Sporadic border incidents and mutual accusations continue under the fragile 2025 ceasefire, but no return to open fighting; watch the NSC's MOU review outcome as a potential flashpoint.

countries.name: Bangladesh
incidents: Ongoing — nationwide: Heightened security alert continued around the banned Awami League's founding anniversary amid intelligence warnings of possible unrest by Sheikh Hasina loyalists.
Ongoing — nationwide: Sporadic political violence continued as part of the broader post-Hasina transition instability ahead of the February 2026 elections' aftermath period.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. Elevated vigilance around political anniversaries continues, but no major violent incident was confirmed in this window.

countries.name: Sri Lanka
incidents: 5-6 Jul — Negombo prison: A major riot between long-term convicts and temporary detainees, linked to drug trafficking, killed at least 26 (including 6 prison officers) and wounded 100+ — the deadliest prison unrest in years.
Ongoing — nationwide: Multiple parallel investigations were launched, including a three-member committee led by a retired apex-court judge, into the riot's causes.
3 Jul — Colombo: The 6th Pakistan-Sri Lanka Armed Forces Defence Dialogue concluded at Navy headquarters.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained, but institutionally significant. The prison riot is a major internal-security/corrections-system failure — the highest death toll in years — though not linked to broader insurgent or geopolitical conflict; monitor the judicial review's findings for systemic reform implications.

regions.name: Americas
overview: Four CRITICAL-tier crises are now interlinked rather than parallel: Venezuela's constitutional vacuum is compounding with a 3,685-dead earthquake disaster; Colombia's transition freeze coincided with the ELN's largest kidnapping in months (39 people, 14 Jul); Cuba suffered three total grid collapses in nine days; Haiti's UNSC review (14 Jul) landed amid a still-understrength international force.

Institutional transitions are the common failure point across the region — Colombia (Petro-to-de la Espriella), Peru (to Fujimori, 28 Jul) and Venezuela are all mid-handover, each producing disputed-legitimacy friction that should be treated as a regional pattern rather than isolated events.

Organized-crime and gang violence remain the dominant non-political threat vector in Ecuador (record Manabí violence), Haiti (Gang Suppression Force vs. gangs), Colombia (ELN) and Peru (an extortion epidemic hitting 1,000+ Lima schools) — outpacing state capacity in each case despite active security operations.

Watch the clustered late-July risk window: 20 July (Colombia protests), 28 July (Venezuela's two-year protest anniversary and Peru's Fujimori inauguration) — all landing within days of each other across three CRITICAL/HIGH-rated countries.

countries.name: Venezuela
incidents: 5 Jul — Caracas: Delcy Rodríguez's constitutionally mandated 180-day interim presidential term expired with no election called, deepening the legitimacy vacuum.

10-12 Jul — Caracas: At a Military Academy graduation for 1,200+ new officers, Rodríguez warned there is 'no room for any kind of conspiracy, either internal or external,' signaling concern over military loyalty.

14 Jul — nationwide: The earthquake death toll climbed to 3,685 (16,740 injured); opposition figure María Corina Machado is reportedly seeking to return and challenge Rodríguez's authority ahead of 28 July protests marking the two-year anniversary of the disputed 2024 election.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, pressure compounding. The constitutional vacuum is now colliding with a mass-casualty earthquake disaster, creating simultaneous legitimacy and humanitarian crises. The 28 July protest date is a hard flashpoint; watch for a security-force crackdown as it approaches and any Machado re-entry attempt.

countries.name: Haiti
incidents: 8-9 Jul — Kenscoff: A gang attack on the town, 20km south of Port-au-Prince, killed several residents and burned multiple homes despite the Gang Suppression Force's presence.
10 Jul — Port-au-Prince: Sri Lanka joined the Gang Suppression Force as its 6th troop-contributing nation; the force remains under 1,000 personnel of its planned 5,500.
14 Jul — New York: The UN Security Council held its scheduled 90-day Haiti briefing, with BINUH and GSF leadership presenting on the mandate and resourcing.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. The GSF's growth to six contributing nations is a positive institutional signal, but force strength remains grossly inadequate against gangs controlling large parts of the capital; the Kenscoff attack shows the force cannot yet protect areas near Port-au-Prince even with foreign troops present.

countries.name: Colombia
incidents: 1-6 Jul — Chocó: The ELN carried out 50+ violent actions, including drone attacks and road blockades, tied to the group's 4 July anniversary.

7 Jul — Bogotá: President-elect Abelardo de la Espriella suspended the presidential transition process, accusing outgoing President Petro of plotting a 'coup' after Petro alleged unsubstantiated election fraud.
8 Jul — Bogotá: Justice Minister Jorge Iván Cuervo resigned, citing disagreement with Petro's 'Total Peace' strategy one month before the term ends.
14 Jul — Chocó (Quibdó-Carmen de Atrato road): The ELN ambushed and kidnapped at least 39 people, including a minor, from two passenger buses; a soldier was killed responding to the scene.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained. Despite Petro's 10 July pledge of a 'peaceful transition,' the transition remains formally suspended and the ELN has escalated from a protest-anniversary campaign to a mass kidnapping of 39 people. Markets remain calm and the 7 August handover is still expected, but the combination of a stalled transition and an active ELN offensive raises real risk of disruption before inauguration.

countries.name: Cuba
incidents: 6 Jul — nationwide: A total national grid collapse left roughly 10 million people without power; public transportation halted and tens of thousands of surgeries were canceled.
10 Jul — nationwide: A second total grid collapse within five days — the 4th of 2026 and 9th since late 2024 — with generation running at under 30% of demand.
14 Jul — nationwide: A third nationwide blackout in under 10 days; the US announced new sanctions on 10 entities including Cuba's Ministry of Tourism, prompting street protests across the island.
countries.coa: CRITICAL maintained, escalating within the band. Three total grid collapses in nine days indicate reserve capacity is effectively gone, meaning any single plant failure now cascades nationally; new US sanctions signal continued rather than easing pressure. Monitor for a fourth collapse and any acceleration of street unrest.

countries.name: Ecuador
incidents: Ongoing (June-July) — Manabí province: At least 63 organized-crime violence events against civilians were recorded in June, the worst month for civilian-targeted violence since tracking began in 2022, as Los Lobos presses its war against a weakened Los Choneros for the Manta/Puerto López trafficking corridor.
Ongoing — coastal provinces: Guayas, Manabí, El Oro, Los Ríos and Esmeraldas continue to account for roughly 88% of national homicides under the government's 'internal armed conflict' designation.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained. No single mass-casualty event was confirmed this week, but the Manabí trend line (worst month on record) indicates sustained, high-intensity gang conflict rather than a lull.

countries.name: Peru
incidents: 2 Jul — 796 districts across 22 departments plus Callao: A 60-day state of emergency was declared ahead of anticipated 2026-27 El Niño rainfall, layered onto an existing Lima/Callao crime-related state of emergency.
Ongoing — Lima: At least 1,000 schools in the capital are reportedly under extortion, with gangs demanding $14,000-$28,000 each; crime is estimated to cost Peru roughly $5 billion annually.
Upcoming, 28 Jul — Lima: Keiko Fujimori is set to be inaugurated president after her June runoff win; anti-Fujimorista protest mobilization is expected given prior clashes at her campaign rallies.
countries.coa: HIGH maintained but watch-listed upward. Dual states of emergency (crime and weather) plus an incoming, polarizing president facing an active extortion epidemic set up a volatile inauguration period on 28 July.

countries.name: Bolivia
incidents: Through 9 Jul — nationwide: The death toll from blockade-related unrest reached 24 killed and 37 injured per Human Rights Watch, including six deaths linked to ambulances blocked by road cuts.
29 Jun — nationwide: The government floated the boliviano, ending its US-dollar peg, amid economic fallout from weeks of unrest.
2 Jul — nationwide: HRW reported roads have been cleared of blockades, but warned the underlying crisis — driven by Law 1720 protests and deep economic strain — persists.
countries.coa: MODERATE maintained. Active blockades have been dispersed, reducing immediate disruption risk, but unresolved political and economic drivers keep renewed unrest plausible; reassess to HIGH if blockade activity resumes at scale.

countries.name: Dominican Republic
incidents: 6-7 Jul — Santo Domingo Norte/La Vega/San José de las Matas: Three women were found killed in separate incidents within 48 hours, spotlighting a gender-violence spike; one suspect was arrested.
11 Jul — Las Américas International Airport: Two Dominican nationals were extradited to the US on drug-trafficking and organized-crime charges.
countries.coa: LOW maintained. No organized armed-conflict or mass-unrest indicators; this week's incidents are criminal/gender-violence and routine counter-narcotics cooperation, consistent with baseline.

countries.name: Jamaica
incidents: 5-11 Jul — nationwide: 16 murders were recorded for the week; the year-to-date total of 291 as of 11 July is 83 fewer than the same period in 2025, a 22% year-on-year decline.
Ongoing — nationwide: Police-involved lethality continued rising even as overall murders fall, a secondary human-rights watch item.
countries.coa: LOW maintained and trending favorably. Homicides continue a fourth consecutive year of first-half declines; monitor the rising police-lethality rate as a separate oversight issue.
